comment:
Woah there. That's quite a lot of misinformation there. Let's clear some things up. 

Not a Callaway fan boy.. I've been extremely critical of Callaway over the years. Golf balls test how they test. The numbers are the numbers and they don't lie. 

I don't know what you mean by Bridgestone super soft. I don't know if that's a typo or if I've just never heard of that golf ball. 

As for the ultisoft, are you in the USA by chance? Because the ultisoft isn't even available over here. You might be able to get it on Amazon sometimes but it's not readily available. 

Here in the USA bridgestone's two-piece mainstream balls the e6. The Callaway super soft is the main two-piece softball from callaway. And the srixon  soft feel is in the same category only from a different brand. These balls are absolutely 100% in the same category and I'm comparing apples to apples.

When I used to play Supersofts, they would always roll through the green in the wet for me. comment:
I feel like maybe I just need to make a video on this. Honestly it's getting tiring having to explain it over and over. 

Every year the reviews are going to be different. I use different swing speeds, different clubs, and sometimes even a different launch monitor. Last year when I tested the super soft I was swinging about 5 to 7 mph faster with completely different clubs. The results are going to be different. I appreciate you calling me out, I'm definitely not perfect. But we have to understand that even when it comes down to basic science you can do the same experiment 10 times and get different results.

Also something to keep in mind. Golf companies will make minor adjustments throughout. Even though a golf ball is the same model in 2023, they will make updates for 2024 even though they are minor. It's more of a quality control issue than anything

comment:
Not only do we have the MGS golf ball test but also the Today's Golfer test.

Both tests are excellent in methodology but utterly hopeless at statistical analysis and crucially never take into account statistically significance.

Let us start with the mantra that "soft is slow" something MGS has been pushing for years with absolutely no statistically significant evidence to back it up.  Both MGS and Today's Golfer need to employ a professional statistician to determine the correct test for statistical significance and apply it to the data. Without that work being done then it is more than likely that ball speed differences from the average are simply testing chance. 

Spin is more interesting.  There are massive differences in wedge spin in the MGS data, not so much in the Today's Golfer data.

But where I agree with MGS, you do not need to spend a fortune to get a great ball.

comment: I have seen a few videos about this TT. I believe I fall into the category of a person who has trouble lining up the lines where it interferes with my feel and focus. Ricky Fowler commented about this very thing in another video. One can adjust head angles and whatnot to line it up with practice. It really all depends on what your preference is and how your brain interprets things I guess. For me, I have learned one extra thing from this to help me with short putts. I line up without TT and do it well with my preferred adjustments. Now I can align to the centre of my target then imagine where the extra two lines are without them being there. I have no problem doing this the way my putter is designed and only use this method for straight putts. For breaking putts I use something completely different and that works pretty well too. I believe the average golfer lines up poorly but believes they are lined up correctly. TT will help these people line it up better. People who have made the adjustments to know they are lined up correctly won’t need it for the most part. Thanks for the video.

Great question! In the video, I showed a chart for swing speed and matching up the compression you need and then in the description of the video, i provide some example golf balls and their compression.

comment: I find lots of golf balls and have tried most of the makes around in the last 5-8 years. Some were not good for my game and some were. I am in the UK where weather conditions are let's say not perfect, windy being the obvious and wet conditions so stopping on the greens is almost guaranteed. Recently I have tried Seed and Trust both very good, but I don't find many of them and I don't buy balls, I have of course but the £40+ is better in my pocket. I play Tp5 because they offer me what I want. They are good in the wind and being soft premium they help me with my draw. There are so many choices, I reckon there is no hard and fast rule, play what you want/can afford. I like the tests but they can only go so far, it's what you like, clicky off the putter ain't for me personally, crap control for that 30 yard pitch or that short sided shot out of the bunker. Distance is not that important for me tbh, 230-270 yards I ain't bothered about I do hit good distance off my irons. It's just personal choice

comment: Great video and data breakdown. Unlike others, I did enjoy the fact that you had a human swinging at the ones that had the best robot data. Robots don’t give feedback. I’ve recently been going through a few different balls looks for the best performance/value ratio for me. Used information from robot tests and human reviews both. The robot data tells you what the “perfect swing” can get. I am far from the perfect swing. Have tried Vice Tour and Pro, Maxfli Tour X, PXG Xtreme, Callaway ERC Soft, and Pro V1. Maxfli was longest off the driver at 275, but I couldn’t get a decent iron or wedge shot. Have found the PXG and Callaway to be the best 2 for me tee to green. Both go around 260 off driver, and I can be more consistent with iron and wedge shots. Best iron shot was a 188 yard 5 iron with the ERC. Keep up the good work!

comment: Compression matters a lot - but what matters as much for hitting irons/hybrids to greens and chipping is the cover. Soft greens can take a cheaper soft ball (Supersoft) and it will stop. In a 2024 compression video, you referenced a chart from Golf Sidekick that lists most (not all) golf balls and their compression.  Great resource.  Thank you!

comment:
Compression ratings are determined by the manufacturer and most manufacturers have different methods of calculating compression. To achieve better results, you could go to the public data for ball tests hit with a robot and measured with a GC Quad or Trackman, and then measure the compression of each ball with a standard compression measuring device. Using your own swing adds a significant unnecessary variable. 

My gut feeling is over 25-50 different balls, all hit of a calibrated robot and measured indoors with a GC Quad or Trackman, is that there would be a significant different between the 2-3 shortest balls and the 2-3 longest balls, but the majority would probably be roughly within five to eight yards of carry distance.

Studies have shown that the most significant variable to shooting lower scores is driving distance, with lower handicap players hitting the ball further. If you don’t need the distance, then a ball with higher spin / trajectory give your clubs and swing, would be the most important.

If you must have a more extreme yardage using a more extreme longer ball, then you will need a good short game to get up and down more often.

Of course, you can also change the tees you play from … but for some players who should play from shorter tees, that is just not an option.

Also, besides comparing using a standardized compression device, would love to see them compared by price as well …

I would really appreciate someone using a standard compression device, measure all the balls, and then compare their robot swing data to better group and characterize the balls.

If we look deeper into the science of it, there are a lot more factors. For example, angle of attach and low point in swing. These relate to how a golfer compresses the ball. So, like you said, a slower swing speed can compress the ball more than a higher swing speed depending on how they swing it 😊 

Always start with the right rating for your speed and there for harder or softer from there depending on what results your seeing on the course. 

Maybe my 2026 update if this topic should mention these things 🤔

comment:
Played the Chrome Tour, Zstar Diamond, and XV today, and I lost all three! Z-Diamond spun so high off the driver and flew right. XV was a little better but flew high right OB on its second drive. Chrome Tour was awesome! Flew straight and about 100ft high. Hit a 7 iron from a (wrong) fairway bunker 175 yards uphill and stuck it 20 ft from the pin! Flew perfectly straight! Wind today and played right after some rain here in Thailand. Hot and HUMID!
Will try the Staff Model tomorrow. Currently, 2023 Triad is my gamer.
